title: Non-destructive cavity readout of molecules for precision measurements
authors: Alejandro Salas-Estrada, Silviu-Marian Udrescu, Geoffrey Zheng, Qian Wang, Arian Jadbabaie, Vladan Vuletić, David DeMille, Ronald F. Garcia Ruiz, Edwin Pedrozo-Peñafiel
abstract: We propose a non-destructive method to measure the population of molecules in a selected rotational-hyperfine state by coupling them to a high-finesse optical cavity. In contrast to traditional techniques, our approach enables fast (less than 1 ms) repeated measurements with reduced heating and losses, and with precision below the standard quantum limit. The method is particularly advantageous for radioactive molecules, systems of high interest for symmetry violation searches, for which production and sample size are limited, and repeated interrogation is essential for improved sensitivity.
summary: The authors present a novel, non-destructive cavity readout technique for measuring molecular quantum states in ultracold molecules like SrF. By monitoring the shift in a high-finesse optical cavity resonance, they can achieve measurement precision below the Standard Quantum Limit. This method is crucial for enabling high-cycle-rate quantum sensing experiments, especially for sensitive species like radioactive molecules.
main_problem: The paper proposes a non-destructive method to measure the population of molecular quantum states for high-precision measurements, particularly for rare or radioactive molecules.
main_result: The technique achieves measurement variances below the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL) on sub-millisecond timescales while minimizing molecular loss, enabling high-duty cycle measurements.
method: The readout utilizes cavity quantum electrodynamics (cavity-QED) by measuring the shift in the high-finesse optical cavity resonance, which is proportional to the molecular population difference.
model_or_system: The system involves ultracold molecules (e.g., SrF) trapped in an optical lattice/MOT and coupled to a high-finesse optical cavity. The measurement relies on dispersive coupling in the far-detuned regime.
key_observables: Cavity resonance shift ($\delta_0$), molecular population difference ($J_z$), and measurement variance ($\Delta J_z$).
important_parameters: Measurement time scale (< 1 ms), Standard Quantum Limit (SQL), single-molecule cooperativity ($\eta$), and molecular loss probability ($p_{	ext{loss}}$).
main_assumptions: The readout is nearly nondestructive, and the measurement uncertainty is derived using the Cramér-Rao bound based on input-output formalism.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the cavity transmission spectrum showing shifts proportional to molecule number, and plots compare the achievable measurement variance against the SQL limit.
paper_structure: The work progresses from establishing the need for non-destructive readout, detailing the cavity-QED measurement formalism (dispersive shift), quantifying the achievable precision relative to the SQL, and finally describing the necessary prerequisite steps like ultra-cold molecule trapping (CB MOT) and state preparation.
why_interesting: This work represents a state-of-the-art experimental realization of quantum sensing using molecular qubits coupled to cavities, directly relevant to open quantum systems and precision measurement protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Molecules interacting strongly with the light inside a high finesse optical cavity (a) Top: The molecules are op- tically trapped (trapping light not shown) close to the beam waist of the light building up inside the cavity (red). An ex- ternally applied magnetic field defines the quantization axis along the cavity (gray arrow). A photodetector captures the reflected light, which provides a dispersive measurement of the molecular state population. Bottom: Relevant energy levels in SrF. The transition |↓⟩→|e⟩is driven using off resonant light (red arrow) with a frequency close to the dressed cavity resonance. The light detuning from the molecular transition is ∆≫Γ. The possible...

FIG. 2. (a) Measurement variance normalized to the SQL as a function of the number of scattered photons per molecule for an ensemble of N = 1000 SrF molecules with detection efficiency q = 0.4. Each curve corresponds to a cavity of length ∼5 cm, mode waist 10 µm, and detuning 1 GHz from the molecular resonance. The finesse ranges from ∼7,000 to 140,000, determining the cooperativity and linewidth. Black points indicate the optimal measurement positions where Ra- man noise equals photon shot noise.
